edges.description: Loss of UBA1 ubiquitin-activating function compromises the downstream ubiquitin-proteasome and ubiquitin-dependent protein homeostasis machinery.
Proteostatic failure in motor neurons drives their degeneration and loss.
Motor neuron loss denervates skeletal muscle, producing congenital hypotonia, areflexia, and profound weakness.
In utero motor neuron loss reduces fetal movement, leading to joint contractures and reduced fetal movement / polyhydramnios.
Phrenic and intercostal motor unit failure compromises ventilation.
Bulbar and generalized weakness impair sucking and swallowing.
Fetal akinesia from motor neuron loss produces multiple congenital joint contractures.
Reduced fetal movement and ossification predispose to perinatal bone fractures.

nodes.description: Degeneration and loss of anterior horn (lower motor) neurons in the spinal cord and brainstem is the defining neuropathological lesion of XL-SMA, producing denervation of skeletal muscle. UBA1 levels and ubiquitin-dependent pathways are critical to motor neuron survival, and impaired UBA1 activity is implicated in motor neuron degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy: clinical definition and molecular mapping.
evidence.snippet: X-linked infantile spinal-muscular atrophy (XL-SMA) is a rare disorder, which presents with the clinical characteristics of hypotonia, areflexia, and multiple congenital contractures (arthrogryposis) associated with loss of anterior horn cells and death in infancy.

X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy (XL-SMA) is characterized by congenital hypotonia, areflexia, and evidence of degeneration and loss of anterior horn cells (i.e., lower motor neurons) in the spinal cord and brain stem.

evidence.reference_title: The pivotal role of ubiquitin-activating enzyme E1 (UBA1) in neuronal health and neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: accumulating evidence implicating impaired UBA1 activity in a range of neurodegenerative conditions, including Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, Huntington's disease and spinal muscular atrophy

evidence.reference_title: X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy: clinical definition and molecular mapping.
evidence.snippet: multiple congenital contractures (arthrogryposis) associated with loss of anterior horn cells and death in infancy
Assure adequate caloric intake by caloric supplementation and/or gastrostomy feedings

nodes.description: Because UBA1 sits at the apex of the ubiquitin cascade, its impairment disrupts all downstream ubiquitylation-dependent processes, including proteasome-mediated protein degradation and protein homeostasis. Disturbed proteostasis is a shared feature of UBA1-linked neurodegeneration and renders motor neurons, which are highly dependent on intact protein quality control, vulnerable to degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting the ubiquitin E1 as a novel anti-cancer strategy.
evidence.snippet: UBA1 is the initial enzyme in the ubiquitination cascade and initiates the transfer of ubiquitin molecules to target proteins where they are degraded by the proteasome.

evidence.reference_title: The pivotal role of ubiquitin-activating enzyme E1 (UBA1) in neuronal health and neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: UBA1 is thus of fundamental importance to the modulation of ubiquitin homeostasis and to all downstream ubiquitylation-dependent cellular processes, including proteolysis through the ubiquitin-proteasome system and selective autophagy.

evidence.reference_title: UBA1: At the Crossroads of Ubiquitin Homeostasis and Neurodegeneration.
evidence.snippet: A common feature of these conditions is disruption of protein homeostasis.
Often congenital contractures and/or fractures are present.
Life span is significantly shortened because of progressive ventilatory insufficiency resulting from chest muscle involvement.

nodes.description: Loss of anterior horn motor neurons during fetal life reduces fetal movement (fetal akinesia). Chronic intrauterine immobility of joints leads to multiple congenital contractures (arthrogryposis), and the akinesia sequence is often heralded by reduced fetal movements and polyhydramnios; reduced ossification and disuse contribute to a tendency to perinatal bone fractures.

evidence.reference_title: X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy: clinical definition and molecular mapping.
evidence.snippet: multiple congenital contractures (arthrogryposis) associated with loss of anterior horn cells
Often congenital contractures and/or fractures are present.

nodes.description: Denervation of skeletal muscle by degenerating lower motor neurons produces the severe congenital hypotonia, areflexia, and profound generalized weakness that define the neonatal presentation, with progressive chest-muscle involvement leading to ventilatory insufficiency, feeding difficulty, and a shortened life span.

evidence.snippet: Life span is significantly shortened because of progressive ventilatory insufficiency resulting from chest muscle involvement.

nodes.description: Hemizygous pathogenic variants in UBA1 (clustered in exon 15 / the active adenylation domain) impair the function of the E1 ubiquitin-activating enzyme, the most upstream enzyme of the ubiquitin cascade that activates ubiquitin before its transfer to E2 conjugating enzymes and E3 ligases. Reduced or altered UBA1 activity is the primary molecular lesion of SMAX2.

evidence.reference_title: Rare missense and synonymous variants in UBE1 are associated with X-linked infantile spinal muscular atrophy.
evidence.snippet: This resulted in detection of three rare novel variants in exon 15 of UBE1 that segregate with disease

evidence.reference_title: A novel UBA1 gene mutation in a patient with infantile respiratory distress syndrome.
evidence.snippet: UBA1 is an E1 ubiquitin-activating enzyme that initiates the ubiquitylation of target proteins and is thus a key component of the ubiquitin signaling pathway.

evidence.reference_title: A novel UBA1 gene mutation in a patient with infantile respiratory distress syndrome.
evidence.snippet: This missense mutation was located with the AAD (active adenylation domain) of the protein, a known hotspot of SMAX2 mutations.
